C# example 0.
Goal: teach how to parse input with a loop.
The example uses a List and a List<int> value.
```cs
static int ProcessCsharp00(IEnumerable<int> items) {
    var total = 0;
    foreach (var value in items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is NullReferenceException.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```cs
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is NullReferenceException.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

The example uses a List and a Task value.
```cs
static int ProcessCsharp0411(IEnumerable<int> items) {
    var total = 0;
    foreach (var value in items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is NullReferenceException.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

The example uses a List and a IEnumerable<string> value.
```cs
static int ProcessCsharp0548(IEnumerable<int> items) {
    var total = 0;
    foreach (var value in items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is NullReferenceException.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```cs
static int ProcessCsharp0593(IEnumerable<int> items) {
    var total = 0;
    foreach (var value in items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is NullReferenceException.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```cs
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is NullReferenceException.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

C# example 864.
Goal: teach how to parse input with a guard clause.
The example uses a Dictionary and a string value.
```cs
static int ProcessCsharp0864(IEnumerable<int> items) {
    var total = 0;
    foreach (var value in items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```

The example uses a Dictionary and a IEnumerable<string> value.
```cs
static int ProcessCsharp01183(IEnumerable<int> items) {
    var total = 0;
    foreach (var value in items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is NullReferenceException.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

The example uses a HashSet and a List<int> value.
```cs
static int ProcessCsharp01228(IEnumerable<int> items) {
    var total = 0;
    foreach (var value in items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is NullReferenceException.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

```cs
static int ProcessCsharp01497(IEnumerable<int> items) {
    var total = 0;
    foreach (var value in items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is NullReferenceException.

```cs
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is NullReferenceException.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

The example uses a HashSet and a Task value.
```cs
static int ProcessCsharp01634(IEnumerable<int> items) {
    var total = 0;
    foreach (var value in items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.
A common mistake in this topic is NullReferenceException.
Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
Keep names descriptive, keep steps small, and test one behavior at a time.

C# example 1680.
Goal: teach how to parse input with a loop.
The example uses a HashSet and a IEnumerable<string> value.
```cs
static int ProcessCsharp01680(IEnumerable<int> items) {
    var total = 0;
    foreach (var value in items) {
        if (value >= 0) total += value;
    }
    return total;
}
```
// Read the code from top to bottom.
The function receives data, skips invalid values, and returns one clear result.

Check the empty input case before trusting the code.
